source_texts: Meanwhile the man in the yellow frock-coat had been fumbling in his waistcoat pocket without anyone noticing. In fact the other travellers were drinking or playing cards and paying no attention to anything else.

As for the man, he truly was so tired that he did not even make the most of those clean white sheets. He snuffed out his candle with his nostril, the way convicts do, and dropping on to the bed fully dressed, immediately fell into a deep sleep.
“Sit down, Ivan, please, here on the sofa,” Alyosha said, frightened. “You’re feverish. Lie down. Put your head on the pillow. Right. Would you like a wet towel on your head? It might help.”
“Where to?” Alyosha whispered back, glancing around him. They were alone in a smallish garden. The house was at least fifty yards away. “Why do you have to whisper?”

How could I possibly have known that he’d come here with his finger half bitten off? Otherwise, really I might have hidden them, just as you say. I see that you have at last come up with a clever remark today.”
Nikolai Apollonovich perceived a shady type (perhaps of the petty bourgeoisie) with his neck wrapped up.
“That is not right, it’s a mistake!” Nina Alexandrovna intervened suddenly, looking almost ruefully at the Prince. “Mon mari se trompe.” *

“It was all thanks to Kamsa that I was able to enter into this city,” said Vandiya Devan and started recounting yet another adventure of his. Vandiya Devan knew that the city would be on alert and guards would be posted at the gates to capture him. They also knew that he would come bearing the Pazhuvur Signet ring so he couldn’t possibly use it to gain entry.
She occupied, in the Boulevard Haussmann, the second storey of a large new house, the owner of which was content to let to single ladies, in order to get his plaster dried.

The wonderful thing about the collision between young minds is that you can never predict the spark or anticipate the flash. What will the next moment bring? No one knows. The burst of laughter succeeds compassion. In the comic instant seriousness comes into play. Impulses are prompted by a chance word. Each individual’s verve is paramount. A joke is enough to create an opening for the unexpected. These are exchanges that take abrupt turns, with sudden realignments of perspective. Fortuitousness is the scene-shifter in such conversations.
rationales: Outputs 2 and 3 share nearly identical vocabulary, phrasing, and structure: both begin with 'At the same time,' both use 'yellow frock-coat,' 'searching in his waistcoat pocket,' 'without anyone noticing,' 'other passengers,' 'drinking or playing cards,' and 'not paying attention to anything else.' The only difference is that output 3 merges the two sentences into one using 'since,' a minor structural variation consistent with the same paraphraser producing slightly different outputs.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the same distinctive phrasing 'He put out his candle with his nostril, as convicts do,' use 'fully clothed,' 'at once,' and 'deep sleep' in parallel constructions. Their sentence structures are nearly identical, with only minor word substitutions (e.g., 'unable even to appreciate' vs. 'did not even take advantage of'), suggesting they come from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 3 share nearly identical structure and word choices: both open with 'Please' followed by Ivan's name, both use 'You have a fever' (rather than 'delirious/delirium'), both have 'Lie [back/down] and rest your head on the pillow,' and both offer a damp/wet towel with a closing line about it making him feel better. The sentence-by-sentence decisions are consistently parallel, indicating the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 3 share an almost identical structure and phrasing pattern: both open with 'Where are we going?' followed by 'murmured [adverb]', both use 'The two of them were by themselves in a [modifier] garden', both use 'The house stood a good fifty yards [location phrase]', and both end with a question about the necessity of whispering. The sentence-by-sentence template is the same, with only minor word substitutions (e.g., 'softly' vs 'quietly', 'modest-sized' vs 'fairly small', 'behind them' vs 'off', 'What's the need for' vs 'What's the reason for').

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical opening phrasing ('How was I supposed to know he would show up here with his finger...'), both use 'hidden them, just as you said/suggest,' and both close with a remark about the interlocutor making a sharp/clever observation. These parallel structural and lexical choices strongly indicate the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the same core vocabulary choices ('lower middle class,' gender-neutral terms 'person'/'individual'), similar sentence structure with a parenthetical/comma aside for the class description, and the same relative clause construction. Output 0 differs by using 'petty bourgeois class' and 'man.' Output 1 is a multi-bullet format from a different system, and Output 3 covers entirely different source content. The closest stylistic fingerprint match is between 2 and 4.

Outputs 0 and 1 are nearly identical in structure, word choice, and style. Both preserve 'Mon mari se trompe,' both use the phrase 'turning/turned to him suddenly,' and both describe the expression as 'almost with/in anguish.' The sentence structure is the same, with only minor tense and punctuation differences (a footnote number vs. a double-dagger symbol), strongly suggesting they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 3 are the most similar in style and structure. Both use nearly identical phrasing: 'He knew the city would be on high alert, with guards stationed at the gates to arrest him.' Both also share the same concise ending about not being able to use the signet ring. The sentence constructions, word choices, and overall length are almost identical, pointing to the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing: both use 'large new building' (not 'newly built'), 'whose owner was willing to rent to', and 'simply to help dry out the...plaster'. The only minor differences are 'single women' vs 'unmarried women' and 'the fresh plaster' vs 'the plaster', suggesting these are from the same paraphraser with slight variation.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the most consistent stylistic fingerprints: both use 'Laughter can follow tenderness' (0: 'without warning', 4: 'in an instant'), both use 'Gravity can emerge/enter from/in the middle of something funny', both phrase the chance-word idea as 'a single word/offhand remark can set off a chain/train of thought', both use 'open the door to something unforeseen/completely unforeseen', and both describe perspective shifts with 'without warning/suddenly and without warning'. The sentence structure, ordering of ideas, and choice of metaphors are remarkably parallel across both outputs, indicating the same paraphraser.

These exchanges are characterized by rapid changes and sudden shifts in viewpoint, with chance often guiding the conversation in new directions.

In the meantime, unnoticed by anyone, the man wearing the yellow frock-coat was rummaging around in his waistcoat pocket. The other passengers were preoccupied with their drinks and card games, taking no notice of anything going on around them.

The man was so exhausted that he did not even take advantage of the clean white sheets. He put out his candle with his nostril, as convicts do, and then, still fully dressed, collapsed onto the bed and at once fell into a deep sleep.

‘Brother, sit down!’ Alyosha said in alarm. ‘ Sit down, for the love of God, here, on the sofa. You are in delirium, rest on the cushion, there, like that. Would you like me to put a wet towel round your head? Perhaps that might make it better?’
Glancing around the small garden where they stood alone, Alyosha whispered back, "Where to?" The house was at least fifty yards away, which prompted the other person to ask why he was whispering.
"But I couldn't tell that he would come with a bad finger, or else perhaps I might have done it on purpose. My darling mamma, you begin to say really witty things."
Nikolai Apollonovich noticed a suspicious-looking individual (possibly from the lower middle class) who had his neck covered up.
"That's wrong, it's an error!" Nina Alexandrovna suddenly cut in, gazing at the Prince with an expression that was almost sorrowful. "Mon mari se trompe." *

Vallavarayan had guessed, quite accurately, that the Pazhuvettarayars' men would be lying in wait for him even before he arrived at Pazhaiyarai himself, guarding every single point of entrance and exit into the city. The least sign of suspicious behaviour, and he would be carted off to prison at once.
She lived on the second floor of a large new building on Boulevard Haussmann, whose owner was willing to rent to unmarried women simply to help dry out the plaster.

What makes the meeting of young minds so remarkable is that you can never foresee what will ignite or where the energy will come from. The next moment remains a mystery. Laughter can follow tenderness in an instant. Gravity can enter right in the middle of something funny. A single word spoken by chance can set off a whole new train of thought. Each person's enthusiasm matters above all else. A well-timed joke can open the door to something completely unforeseen. These conversations take sharp, unexpected turns, with perspectives shifting suddenly and without warning. In exchanges like these, chance itself acts as the one pulling the strings.
